rationale: The citing paper uses this source in its theory-building section to support the core process-versus-outcome distinction, noting prior mental simulation work showing consumers can focus on process or outcome. It then explicitly states that its process/outcome learning mindset concept is developed from this prior stream and uses that logic to derive hypotheses about appeal effectiveness. Although cited alongside another paper, the role is clear and contributes to the conceptual foundation rather than a passing literature list mention.
relation_description: The citing paper draws on the cited paper's process-versus-outcome mental simulation idea as a conceptual basis for defining process/outcome learning mindsets in education consumption. It uses that foundation to theorize how power distance belief shifts preference for cognitive versus affective appeals.
